script: The Supreme Court took away the president's tariff gun — and he's already pointing a new one he doesn't legally own. SCOTUS killed broad executive tariff power in February. This week, Trump threatened 50% tariffs on Iran's arms suppliers with no named legal authority — a bluff aimed at China, timed for a summit where the real negotiations haven't started yet. Meanwhile, the EPA repealed the scientific finding that made all federal climate regulation lawful. Not a deregulatory tweak — an attempt to retroactively void 15 years of environmental law, and courts will now have to decide if that's allowed. In Asia, China cut off the world's supply of the semiconductor material that AI data centers run on, while simultaneously inviting Taiwan's opposition party to Beijing — splitting the island's politics two weeks before Trump meets Xi. Two threads, one through-line: executive authority is collapsing at home while the strategic competition abroad keeps accelerating. The one thing that changes everything tomorrow: whether federal courts treat the EPA endangerment repeal as an administrative action — or a constitutional crisis.
